content.full_text:
Cii-u
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES
COMMISSION
June 30,
1995
Mark Bogetich,
Consultant
Assemblymember Jim Cunneen
California State Legislature
P.O.
Box 942849
Sacramento,
CA
94249—0001
Re:
Your Request for Informal Assistance
Our File No.
1—95-180
Dear Mr.
Bogetich:
This is in response to your request for advice under the
travel provisions of the Political Reform Act.
1
!
Since your
letter did not provide all pertinent facts, it appears that your
request is one of general assistance.
We therefore consider your
letter a request for informal assistance.
2
!
QUESTION
Will the Act’s gift limits prevent you from accepting a gift
of travel,
lodging and subsistence to participate in plant tours,
panel discussions and executive briefings during a three—day visit
to the Silicon Valley?
CONCLUSION
The payments you receive for travel,
lodging and subsistence
for this three—day event will be reportable on your statement of
economic interests if valued at $50 or more.
If valued at $280
or more, the payments are a prohibited gift under the Act.
Government Code Sections 81000—91015.
All statutory
references are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.
Commission regulations appear at
2 California Code of Regulations,
Sections 18000—18954.
All references to regulations are to Title
2,
Division
6 of the California Code of Regulations.
2/
Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the
immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice.
(Government Code Section 83114;
2 Cal.
Code of Regs.
Section
18329(c) (3) .)
42SJSrI,!SI!J
‘‘-


File No.
1—95—180
Page
2
FACTS
You are the consultant for the Select Committee on High
Technology.
As part of your objective to work with high
technology industries in the state,
you have planned a three—day
visit to the Silicon Valley, paid for by an unnamed high
technology industry association.
The visit will include plant
tours at computer and semiconductor facilities, executive
briefings by industry representatives,
and panel discussions of
trade and tax issues affecting the industry.
You stated in your
letter you will bean active participantin each of the events
during your visit.
You also stated that expenses incurred during
the visit will be limited to the day immediately preceding, the
days of,
and the day immediately following the visit.
ANALYSIS
Section 89505 provides that no member of a state board or
commission,
and no designated employee of a state agency, shall
accept gifts with a total value of more than two hundred eighty
dollars
($280)
in a calendar year from any single source, if the
member or employee would be required to report the receipt of
income or gifts from that source on his or her statement of
economic interest. /
As a consultant for the California State Assembly,
you are
designated in the conflict of interest code and are required to
disclose all income from sources located in,
doing business in,
or
planning to do business in, the State of California.
In
accordance with Section 89505,
you are prohibited from receiving
gifts valued at $280 or more.
Certain general exceptions apply to the receipt of gifts.
For instance,
free admission,
and refreshments and similar non—
cash nominal benefits provided to a filer during the entire event
at which the filer gives a speech, participates in a panel or
seminar,
or provides a similar service,
and actual intrastate
transportation and any necessary lodging and subsistence provided
directly in connection with the speech, panel,
seminar, or
service,
including but not limited to meals and beverages on the
day of the activity,
are not payments and need not be reported by
any filer.
(Regulation 18950.3.)
You stated that the three-day visit to the Silicon Valley is
for the purpose of plant tours, executive briefings by industry
representatives,
and panel discussions of trade and tax issues
The gift limitation amounts in this section shall be adjusted
biennially by the commission to reflect changes in the Consumer
Price Index,
rounded to the nearest ten dollars.
(Sections
89505(b),
89504(d).)
Effective January 1,
1995, the gift limits
were adjusted to $280.


File No.
1—95—180
Page
3
affecting the industry.
You indicated in your letter that you are
trying to put this three-day event together.
It is not the
industry executives who are sponsoring a conference,
symposium or
seminar for which you have been invited to make a speech, or sit
as a panel of experts before an audience.
It does not appear from the facts provided that you will be
making a speech before an audience at a conference,
nor does it
appear from the facts that the panel discussion will be at a
conference or seminar.
It appears more likely that you are taking
plant tours
4 / and meeting in private with industry executives
regarding issues of interest to the industry.
There is nothing in
the facts provided which indicate that this activity is taking
place during a symposium,
conference or seminar.
It should be
noted that the exceptions in Regulation 18950.3 contemplate that
the filer will provide a certain level of service at a structured
event.
(Hoehn Advice Letter,
No.
1-92-392.)
Accordingly, the exception for speeches, panels,
or seminars
in Regulation 18950.3 is not met.
If the payments for travel,
lodging,
or subsistence for this three—day event are $50 or more,
the payments must be reported to your next statement of economic
interests.
In addition, the Act’s gift limits prohibit receipt of
gifts of travel if valued at $280 or more.
You should also be aware that if the value of this gift is
$280 or more,
you may not make, participate in making,
or use your
official position to influence a governmental decision which will
have a material financial effect, distinguishable from the effect
on the public generally,
on the source of this gift.
(Section
87103(e).)
The means you could not prepare committee analysis of a
legislative bill,
or advise or recommend a course of action to a
legislative member or his or her staffers, if the source of this
4’
Payments made for on—site demonstrations, tours,
or
inspections designed specifically for the purpose of assisting you
in the performance of your official duties,
although not a speech,
a panel or seminar,
or a similar service covered by Regulation
18950.3, are exempt from the definition of “gift” as
informational.
(Regulation 18942.1.)
However, travel,
lodging
and subsistence given to you in connection with a “plant tour”
does not fall within the exception and must be valued and reported
accordingly.


File No.
1—95—180
Page
4
gift will be materially affected by that legislative bill or
decision.
(Regulation 18700.)
If in the future you have a question regarding a possible
conflict of interest,
you should call or write this office for
guidance prior to acting.
Sincerely,
Steven C.
Churchwell
General Counsel
By:
Jeanette E. Turvill
Political Reform Consultant
Legal Division
SGC/JET/jt
